To be honest now that I look at this I don't understand the rationale behind `writeable`. Some questions:

- I'm probably missing something but given the buffer issue described in #83, shouldn't this (checking if fd is writeable) be done in all `send*` methods *before* the actual `socket.send*` call?
- I noticed `writeable()` is not documented and not used  anywhere except in a unit test. What's the use case of `writeable()` from a user standpoint?

On 12/20/16, giampaolo <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> My point is that modern disks should be fast enough so that the blocking
> time of a single `sendfile()` or `file.read()` call does not represent a
> problem for the main IO loop.

"Disks are fast" is a subjective decleration. I personally
would not like the idea of blocking "Whole Curio"
(including portions which have nothing to do with disk io")
when doing disk io.

And note that native sendfile will block anything while reading
AND SENDING TO THE SOCKET. This will not give back
control until ALL fd is send through the socket. That a lot of time.
Till then you can't be doing anything.

Knowing this indirectly you are relating this to threading.
If native sendfile will be used then there aren't many choices
other than threads.

Or if you are happy with serving single file at a time than that's
ok. But note that if receiving side hangs or is sluggish, all your server
is bricked.

For the upgrade, I'd say it might be worth just spinning up a blank virtualenv/folder and running `generate_will_project`, so you can see how it's laid out now.  The new, config-based version should be backwards-compatible with the old pure-env approach, but imo moving to a config.py makes config and maintenance much simpler.

As for joining when new rooms are created, from here, the implementation path seems reasonably clear:
1. Have will get alerts when new rooms are created.
2. Check that room against the list of rooms he knows to join (including all)
3. Join if appropriate.

So, the referenced issue is simply noting how this backwards compatibility with older notebooks/iPython versions is making things a bit messy and hard to deal with. The suggestion is that as it is too much of pain to support the old versions while trying to add support for the new ones that we instead should simply make breaking changes and start supporting only the new ones. In short, that support does not exist.

That aside, I agree that it would be nice to use v4 directly. However, as you have noted, we have no problem supporting v3 so if you can convert your notebook back into that format everything works fine. Currently, I use v4 notebooks in production (independent of this project) and that is also how I solve this issue for now.

With what you've described above, here's an ideal situation:

1. User runs `git repo config` for the first time and elects to use GitHub.
2. Wizard prompts user for username and password.
3. Two options:

a. User **with** 2FA enabled sends username and password. Wizard receives `X-GitHub-OTP: required; :2fa-type` header (see [here](https://developer.github.com/v3/auth/#working-with-two-factor-authentication)). User is prompted for OTP password, which is sent, in the `X-GitHub-OTP` header, along with another request containing the username and password. GitHub approves, and the wizard continues as normal.
b. User **without** 2FA enabled sends username and password. GitHub approves, and the wizard continues as normal.

This way, anyone with 2FA enabled (i.e. those who have any sense of security) can take advantage of the wizard *and* `git-repo` as a whole.

Its a bit of a digression, but below I did a test inheriting from Tinydb's Document class.  Just interesting for me at least. It does go to idea of being able to specify your own Document class to tinydb though.  If you inherit from the Document class, call the super and was able too assign your subclassed class to tinydb as the Document, would be no need to do what I did below after reading the data back in from the db (create new objects).  I am not sure if you could do the same with custom storage/middleware.  Anyway, Its just an idea. I haven't tried to use it for anything practical yet, just simple tests.  

```python
from tinydb import TinyDB
from tinydb.database import Document

from datetime import datetime as datetime

from faker import Faker
fake = Faker()

class PersonClass(Document):
    '''
    value is a dict
    
    doc_id is used internally by tinydb.  But we need to pass an Integer
    to be able to call the super.__init__ on the Document class.
    When creating these objects(from sratch), I am passing any int, could be 0.
    When creating these objects from data read back from the db, the doc_id,
    has some meaning as long as you pass the doc_id from the recovered database
    doc. 
    '''
    def __init__(self, value, doc_id, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(value, doc_id, **kwargs)
    
    @property
    def full_name(self):
        '''
        returns the full name of the person
        '''
        return "{} {}".format(self['first'], self['last'])
    
    @property
    def some_date_dt(self):
        '''
        The date stored in the db is a string as you have to.
        Just getting a datetime object here. Could help with
        serialisation if you dont need generic code. 
        '''
        the_date = datetime.strptime(self['some_date'], "%Y-%m-%d")
        return the_date
        
    @classmethod
    def from_tinydb_doc(cls, obj):
        '''
        Create a PersonClass from a tinydb doc obj
        Not required for this simple test, but I am just practicing
        doing it.
        '''
        return PersonClass(obj, obj.doc_id)         
    

def make_dummy_people(num_items=100):
    '''
    Create some dummy people to use...
    returns a list of PersonClass objects 
    The data built up using Faker
    '''
    
    return [
            PersonClass(
                {'idx': i,
                'first': fake.first_name(),
                'last': fake.last_name(),
                'age': fake.random_int(min=18, max=106),
                'some_date':fake.date(pattern="%Y-%m-%d"),
                },
                i)
            for i in range(0, num_items)
            ]


db_fn = 'doc_test.json'                  # database filename
dummy_data = make_dummy_people(10)

db = TinyDB(db_fn)                       # create TDB instance w/filename
db.purge_tables()                        # clear all the tables, just for testing
db.insert_multiple(dummy_data)           # add the dummy data to the new table

for doc in db.all():
    # creating PersonClass objects from the doc obj's
    # returned from tinydb.  Its double work, but could be worth it
    # for flexibilty.  With this simple example, very easy to re-create
    # your own document class from the db.
    
    # below line is commented, can use a normal creation or the
    # from_tinydb_doc class method below.  Just trying different things
    #person_doc = PersonClass(doc, doc.doc_id)
    
    # try a classmethod, to create the PersonClass
    person_doc = PersonClass.from_tinydb_doc(doc)
    
    # just print some stuff to show it works
    print(person_doc.doc_id, person_doc.full_name, person_doc.some_date_dt)
    
db.close()

```

When you call synchronous web service the service processes the request and return HTTP status code 200 OK (1) if everything went as expected, or error 4xx. The call is blocked while processing and the request and can take significant time.

When web service is asynchronous the main difference is that call should return instantly with HTTP 202 ACCEPTED (2) which means that request is taken in queue but not processed yet.

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/4228387/what-does-it-mean-when-a-web-service-is-asynchronous

All that the luma library does really is twofold:
* provides a mechanism for displaying a PIL image - it translates that to the correct commands/data for the initialized display device
* give you a PIL-based drawing canvas that is sized for the screen you have initialized. All the drawing and text rendering capabilities are those in [ImageDraw](http://pillow.readthedocs.io/en/4.3.x/reference/ImageDraw.html).

So the short answer is yes (IMHO) - it is a good place to start, but in actual fact, you would be doing most of the graphics drawing using PIL methods.

I’d guess this is because “Fullscreen” in games (versus “Borderless Window” or the like) uses “Exclusive Mode”/“Active Rendering” wherein the application itself works directly with the GPU to write to the screen, as opposed to to working with the window manager.

It’s not impossible to take screenshots _per se_, but you won’t be able to do so through the window manager APIs. In the case of video games (or anything using DirectX in exclusive mode) it’s usually done through DLL injection to hook into DirectX drawcalls.

(A cursory examination of `windows.py` appears to indicate as well that only capturing of the _first_ screen is supported as well, owing to the hardcoded argument to `ctypes.windll.user32.GetWindowDC`.)

Ok, so... here's what I remember, with a quick revision from https://docs.python.org/devguide/setup.html#windows-compiling :
- python 2.7 is built with visual studio 2008. You can get visual studio 2008 from https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/download/details.aspx?id=44266 Whether that's what I used, I'm thinking through as I write
- python 3.5 is build using visual studio 2015. which is why i chose it. And per the link above.
- if I remember rightly, what I do is:
    - deepcl itself is built as a separate dll, using msvc2015.  Its a c++ project, that builds using msvc2015, to a dll
    - the api potentially separates one msvc runtime from another
    - specifically, I put a fair amount of effort into making sure objects on one side of the c-interface are never freed on the other side, nor are they introspected/used on the other side: just treated as a black box
- then, the python bits are compiled with the relevant compiler:
    - python 3.5 cython bits are built using msvc 2015
    - python 2.7 cython bits are built using msvc 2008
- to figure out whether everything I've written above is true or not, or a bit, or not at all, the reference documentation really is: the jenkins build scripts, ie, https://github.com/hughperkins/DeepCL/tree/master/jenkins
    - if you're building python 2.7 on 64-bit windows, then the approprirate script is:  https://github.com/hughperkins/DeepCL/blob/master/jenkins/win64-py27.bat

Lets work through waht it does, bit by bit:

https://github.com/hughperkins/DeepCL/blob/master/jenkins/win64-py27.bat
```
call %~dp0win-py.bat 64 27
```
Ok. First parameter is bitness, 64 bit or 32bit. Second parameter is python version, 2.7 or 3.5. Let's look at win-py.bat: https://github.com/hughperkins/DeepCL/blob/master/jenkins/win-py.bat
```
set bitness=%1
set pyversion=%2
```
ok, so now `bitness` will be `64, and `pyversion` will be 27. Continuing:
```
call %~dp0win-cpp.bat %bitness%
```
This will build the c++ deepcl bit, using msvc 2015. This is the non-python bit.  This is basically the whole of c++ deepcl, so this single line will take a while.  Then, we have:
```
call \py%pyversion%-%bitness%\scripts\activate
```
This is going to activate the relevant python virtual environmetn. In this case it will be a 64-bit python 2.7 virtuale nevironemtn.

Then we have:
```
cd python
python setup.py build_ext -i
```
So this is going to build the python wrappers, using cython. It will use whatever compiler was used to compile the underlying virtual environemtn, which in this case is msvc 2008, and it will use that.

So, we have three 'bricks':
- deepcl c++ brick: msvc 2015
- python 3.5 wrappers: msvc 2015
- python 2.7 wrappers: msvc 2008

To what extent is this somewhat consistent with your experiences above?

From [ODBC API docs](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms713605(v=vs.85).aspx) on `SQL_ATTR_LOGIN_TIMEOUT`:

> ... the number of seconds to wait for a login request to complete before returning to the application.

 I don't quite understand why this doesn't timeout for certain connection problems, e.g. firewall block like in @ltoshea s case, or when server sends no response. But it doesn't, and users who have to deal with unreliable connections will sometimes experience indefinite blocks, instead of the expected timeout.

This makes error handling difficult; I see also users struggling with this in #11.

I replaced `LOGIN` with `CONNECTION` attribute [in this block](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/blob/bc1eec863c5ab2ac6826f72be255661e01bac41e/src/connection.cpp#L74-L78), which did successfully timeout for me testing with FreeTDS against a server which accepts the TCP connection but never responds.

My suggestion would be to expose both `SQL_ATTR_LOGIN_TIMEOUT` and `SQL_ATTR_CONNECTION_TIMEOUT` attributes, perhaps by adding a `connection_timeout` argument to `pyodbc.connect`. This way pyodbc doesn't have to make this choice for the user.

Another suggestion might be to also set `SQL_ATTR_CONNECTION_TIMEOUT` during login, *but reset it afterwards*, keeping the old behaviour of setting `cnxn.timeout=4` so that this doesn't affect queries.

What do you think @mkleehammer ?

On Python 3, os.urandom() returns a bytes value, not a string. So you'd have to decode it to a string using some encoding. However, this doesn't make sense because you're bound to have un-decodable values no matter what encoding you use. For a good explanation about just what bytes and strings are at a conception level, I recommend this talk by Ned Batchelder: http://nedbatchelder.com/text/unipain.html

On Python 2, os.urandom() returns a string, so pyperclip.copy() should work.

The doc for `statuses/filter` is [here](https://dev.twitter.com/streaming/reference/post/statuses/filter). In there, there's a [link](https://dev.twitter.com/streaming/overview/request-parameters#locations) that explains the `locations` parameter. 

If you read that you will see that those four numbers are longitudinal/latitudinal pairs that describe the bounding box of your location. Twitter interprets the first pair as the southwest corner and the second pair and the northeast corner. Your first pair has a longitude of -71, and your second pair has a longitude of -72. This is not valid because -71 is *east* of -72.  Just switch these numbers and it will work.

DTZ values are half-moves until the next **capture, or pawn move, or to mate** while keeping the optimal outcome under the 50-move rule. Captures or pawn moves guarantee that progress is beeing made. (There is only a limited number of them.)

DTZ is in some ways superior to DTM: Imagine a position where you already played 99 half-moves since the last capture or pawn move. Say you can either capture a piece or deliver mate in 2. Then DTM would be 2, but following the DTM line allows your opponent to claim a draw under the 50-move rule. If the capture leads to a winning position DTZ would be 1, and capturing the piece to reset the 50-move counter would indeed be optimal play.

On the other hand DTZ-optimal play is sometimes a bit unnatural. For example here the useless Qg5+ forces a capture as soon as possible while still keeping the win in hand: https://syzygy-tables.info/?fen=8/8/8/7k/K7/R7/3Q4/8_w_-_-_0_1

Oh no! The integration checks are failing. The reason seems to be that I'm depending on a relatively new version of `setuptools`, [which fixed this issue](https://bitbucket.org/pypa/setuptools/src/4249669040239bff20196b17ccd4d73870d37343/CHANGES.txt?fileviewer=file-view-default#cl-10).

It was very hard to distribute a python2/3 cython extension before that change. There were two ways to distribute your package:

 - either just require that the user always has cython installed;
 - or ship the .c file that cython generated in your source distribution. Whenever cython is not available, fall back to that.

Unfortunately, the second option can't be simultaneously python2 and python3 compatible, because cython generates different C sources for each.

The newer version of setuptools allows you to just specify `setup_requires=["cython"]` and you're all set.

Releasing the GIL isn't useful as the histogram calculation is too fast for context switches to be worthwhile. My experience with NumExpr is that even with p-threads you're looking at about 2-5 us per-thread to pass the thread barrier.

Bypassing the Python checks to call the C-API directly is the best speed-up I found:

    Python call histogram average: 7.928e-06 +/- 4.195e-08 s
    C-API call histogram f32 average: 5.724e-06 +/- 2.548e-08 s
    C-API call histogram f64 average: 5.795e-06 +/- 4.494e-08 s

The only other thing that strikes me as a possible optimization would be SIMD vectorizing the loops. Neither MSVC or GCC are vectorizing the loops as they are now (which is sort of obvious both from the uncompelling float32 benchmarks and the compiler messages).  The calls to `ix = (int)((x[i] - xmin) * normx * fnx);` could be vectorized, whereas the accumulations would have to be unrolled manually for the SIMD vector length.   I don't know that it's worth the bother to implement.

Test code:

    import numpy as np
    import fast_histogram
    from time import perf_counter as pc

    tries = 50
    stackDepth = 10000
    nBins = 256
    imSize = 4096
    data = np.random.normal(size=(imSize**2)).reshape(imSize, imSize)
    data32 = data.astype('float32')
    minVal = data.min();  maxVal = data.max()
    print(data.flags)

    times_direct = np.zeros(tries)
    times_direct32 = np.zeros(tries)
    times_single = np.zeros(tries)
    times_overhead = np.zeros(tries)
    # Call C-API directly:
    for I in range(tries):
        t0 = pc()
        for chunk in range(stackDepth):
            _ = fast_histogram._histogram_core._histogram1d(data, nBins, minVal, maxVal)
        times_direct[I] = pc() - t0

    # Call C-API 32-bit version directly:
    for I in range(tries):
        t0 = pc()
        for chunk in range(stackDepth):
            _ = fast_histogram._histogram_core._histogram1d_f32(data32, nBins, minVal, maxVal)
        times_direct32[I] = pc() - t0    

    # Call Python API:
    for I in range(tries):
        t0 = pc()
        for chunk in range(stackDepth):
            _ = fast_histogram.histogram1d(data, nBins, (minVal, maxVal))
        times_single[I] = pc() - t0

    times_single /= stackDepth
    times_overhead /= stackDepth
    times_direct /= stackDepth
    times_direct32 /= stackDepth

    print( f'Python call histogram average: {times_single.mean():.3e} +/- {times_single.std():.3e} s' )
    print( f'C-API call histogram f32 average: {times_direct32.mean():.3e} +/- {times_direct32.std():.3e} s')

Aha!  Simple answer for why scan_8 was failing -- the result saturated.  Meaning, 8-bits wasn't enough to hold the eventual score of 2872 which is much bigger than even an unsigned byte can represent at 255.

I don't exactly have an elegant solution for you.  Here's your code again, but with a check for saturation:

```Python
import parasail
from time import time

s1 = ""
s2 = ""

start = time()
user_matrix = parasail.matrix_create("ACGT", 2, -1)
result = parasail.nw_trace_scan_8(s1, s2, 2, 0, user_matrix)
total_elapsed = time() - start
if result.saturated:
    print("SATURATED!")
else:
    print("scan_8", result.score, result.cigar.seq, result.cigar.decode)
    print("parasail trace scan_8", total_elapsed)

start = time()
user_matrix = parasail.matrix_create("ACGT", 2, -1)
result = parasail.nw_trace_scan_16(s1, s2, 2, 0, user_matrix)
total_elapsed = time() - start
print("scan_16", result.score, result.cigar.seq, result.cigar.decode)
print("parasail trace scan_16", total_elapsed)
```

Basically, if the result saturates, you can't use it.  I don't issue any helpful warnings when it happens because I wanted to leave it up to the user to decide.  On the one hand, I also provide the `_sat` routines that first attempt the 8-bit solution and then automatically detect for saturation and retry with the 16-bit solution.  Unless you are *sure* your score will be <= 127, you are better off using the 16-bit routine to avoid any wasted computation.  The score limit there would be 32,767.  And of course 32-bit would be able to hold a score of 2,147,483,647.  Don't be afraid to do both, use the 16-bit routines *and* check for saturation.  The saturation check is quick, it just checks for a bit flag in the C result.

I will go ahead and close this issue since it seems the code is performing as designed, even if it did cause you some trouble since it is poorly documented.  Remember to consider what your alignment scores will be and then choose the right bit size for the answer.  Beware the performance tradeoffs, i.e. 32-bit is slower than 16-bit.

`minLength` and `maxLength` are not the only properties defined by JSON Schema Draft 4.

Other fields include `pattern` for regex matching, `required` to specify which fields within an object are necessary to pass validation, and `multipleOf` for numeric values.

Take a look at this part of the spec for more info:
https://github.com/json-schema/json-schema/blob/master/draft-04/schema#L45-L69

It would be great if Flask-RestPlus models could implement also these properties for input validation.

In the meantime I'm using custom JSON schema validation like this:

```python
validate_api_key_request = api.json_schema_model('Validate key request', {
    'type': 'object',
    'properties': {
        'user': {'type': 'string', 'minLength': 3, 'maxLength': 32},
        'key': {'type': 'string', 'pattern': '[0-9A-F]{64}'},
    },
    'required': ['user', 'key']
})
```

This is made possible by adding an additional method onto the RestPlus Api class.
This is a bit of a hack, as it will only work for a json schema of an `object`, which defines `properties` and it can only be used for input validation, not for marshalling output.

```python
from flask.ext.restplus import fields
from flask.ext.restplus import Api

class MyApi(Api):
    def json_schema_model(self, name=None, json_schema=None, **kwargs):
        fields_dict = dict([(key, fields.BaseField) for key in json_schema.get('properties', {}).keys()])
        model = self.model(name, fields_dict, **kwargs)
        model.__schema__ = json_schema
        return model
```

If you wanted to allow custom validation by JSON schema, I'm sure you could make this solution more robust.

It looks like the node-semver behaviour is to silently mangle, so I think that'd be best:

```js
> semver.satisfies('1.0.0+somebuild', '>=1.0.0+somebuild')
true
> semver.satisfies('1.0.0+somethingelse', '>=1.0.0+somebuild')
true
> semver.satisfies('1.0.0', '>=1.0.0+somebuild')
true
> semver.satisfies('1.0.0', '>1.0.0+somebuild')
false
> semver.satisfies('1.0.0+somebuild', '>1.0.0+somebuild')
false
> semver.satisfies('1.0.0+zzz', '>1.0.0+somebuild')
false
> semver.satisfies('1.0.1', '>1.0.0+somebuild')
true
```

This makes sense to me as there's no potential ambiguity, the ordering is well defined by the specification: it isn't really coercing in the same way that coercing 1.0 to 1.0.0 is.

AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'path'
```

As you can see, it is essentially random and depends on the order of GC. I think the root of all of these are the fact that you're attempting to do too much in `__del__()` -- by definition the system is in an unpredictable state at this point and you cannot rely on any sane state to do too much work at this point.  From https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html: 
> **Warning** Due to the precarious circumstances under which `__del__()` methods are invoked, exceptions that occur during their execution are ignored, and a warning is printed to sys.stderr instead. Also, when `__del__()` is invoked in response to a module being deleted (e.g., when execution of the program is done), other globals referenced by the `__del__()` method may already have been deleted or in the process of being torn down (e.g. the import machinery shutting down). For this reason, `__del__()` methods should do the absolute minimum needed to maintain external invariants. Starting with version 1.5, Python guarantees that globals whose name begins with a single underscore are deleted from their module before other globals are deleted; if no other references to such globals exist, this may help in assuring that imported modules are still available at the time when the `__del__()` method is called.

> I think this is why @ellisonbg pointed toward implementing env vars entirely in `traitlets.config` rather than in the base traitlets, ...

I have carefully structured this PR to cover functionality all 2 layers (<==>modules): traitlet, config, application;  i believe it's not possible to achieve full *env-var* functionality by affecting any one layer alone.  Actually, traitlets alone support env-vars, without config-layer involvement (i.e. `tag(config=False)`).


> ... because to implement the priority correctly, env handling really needs to be a fundamental part of constructing the config object, and not handled by traits directly.
> ...
> - change how config is constructed from env (populate the config object at application launch time from env variables in between config files and CLI options)

At some point I contemplated going down that road - but stopped couldn't think of all possible use-cases.  `Config` instances are swiss-army knifes, and fitting-in "magic" functionality seemed easier in the `trait/_load_config()` logic.  I'm not equipped to judge this, and not that familiar with the code to implement it here.  But in principle i don't disagree either.


> - implement env only at the traits level, and only simplify the default traits constructor (always lower priority than config), which people have already been doing for years

People can continue doing this, and nothing here will get in their way.  This PR is for something different, env having *higher priority* than configs.


> change how config objects are loaded, so that a series of config objects are stored, with clear priority  ...

Yes, I know, I've already started doing something similar in my app for "runtime" configs stored in different files, etc,  But as you said, too ambitious.  I aimed for the low hanging fruit:

    JUPYTER_NOTEBOOK_DIR=/opt/foo jupyter notebook


> There is precisely one, narrow use case not met by this: overriding already-specified config with environment variables. Since this is already something that doesn't exist, I don't think it's a big loss given the complexity of implementing it.

Well, this use-case, having env-var for values already in config-files, is pretty-common, as shown just above.
If we don't cover this, this PR won't worth its weight.

Still, i haven't understand what is the penalty for hooking in the new functionality in the config layer?

We also have encountered exactly that: instead of failing the task and happily exiting the flow, worker keeps waiting for something to happen.

The tasks with usual failures are handled fine and marked as "disabled". This allows to end the flow with a failure. This works ok. 

But sometimes there is another kind of failure, e.g. with configuration. Then the task's status becomes "unknown", the upstream tasks are "pending" and nothing is happening. So we have to go to the machine and manually restart the worker. One particular example when it happens is when we forget a training slash in `S3FlagTarget`.

* for each operation for a collection

* If we don't have a pagination config, *then* check if it looks pageable.  If it does then we can error out.

I know the original PR was hacky, it was a proof-of-concept patch just to see if it is potentially acceptable.

I have updated the PR with a better implementation and some doc, but let us see if the following behavior is acceptable before working on a test:

```diff
+    # if MONKEYTYPE_MODULES is defined, type only specified packages
+    if 'MONKEYTYPE_MODULES' in os.environ:
+        # try to remove lib_path to only check package and module names
+        for lib_path in LIB_PATHS:
+            try:
+                filename = filename.relative_to(lib_path)
+                break
+            except ValueError:
+                pass
+        return any(x == filename.stem or x in filename.parts for x in os.environ['MONKEYTYPE_MODULES'].split(','))
+    else:
+        return not any(_startswith(filename, lib_path) for lib_path in LIB_PATHS)
```

Basically,
1. The new variable is called `MONKEYTYPE_MODULES` and allows multiple comma separated module names.
2. In contrast to the original `or` logic where non-site packages are always allowed, **only** specified modules will be typed in this updated PR.
3. The implementation strips `LIB_PATHS` but allows checking any of `package`, `sub-package`, or `module` in filename `lib_path/package/sub-package/module.py`.

@tanaysoni as you can see from the backlog here, a reliable reproduction case for this issue has been somewhat hard to come by. #701 was a specific case of this issue, which might be more general than that. On the other hand, it's possible that it's totally solved. @amitripshtos @dmitry-saritasa @nursejason does anyone have evidence that this issue has been resolved on current master?

Here's a theory: say we have a three-node cluster running a topic with RF=2. When we lose one of those nodes, the cluster still contains enough nodes to fully satisfy the replication factor. Fetch requests can continue returning messages, which means [this return statement](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/20db2ac88ac7a4f5710b41eebb8f3c8ab17827da/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L357) can be reached. On the subsequent call to `consume()`, [`_raise_worker_exceptions`](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/20db2ac88ac7a4f5710b41eebb8f3c8ab17827da/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L345) is called and the `SocketDisconnectedError` is raised normally.

Now say that we have a two-node cluster running a topic with RF=2. In this case, when we lose a node, the cluster does not contain enough nodes to fully satisfy the replication factor. Fetch requests cannot return messages anymore (since Kafka's RF guarantee is no longer maintained), which means [this case](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/20db2ac88ac7a4f5710b41eebb8f3c8ab17827da/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L358) will be hit repeatedly. Since the consumer is still `_running` and `block` and `consumer_timeout_ms` are set appropriately, the `while True` loop will spin indefinitely despite the `SocketDisconnectedError` encountered on the fetcher thread.

Assuming that logic is sound, [this pull request](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/pull/321) should fix it. Note that this simply fixes a bug in the existing exception-raising logic of the consumer - it doesn't yet make the consumer able to handle a broker going down without client code catching the exception.

Pykafka has been carrying around a peculiar [bugfix](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/protocol.py#L1516) for a while that caused version 0 of this request to never be used, instead always using v1. To maintain this behavior, [this line](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/814263f5cbf3d4a5caf061b3faafd0d2785cfd15/pykafka/protocol.py#L2415) and [this line](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/814263f5cbf3d4a5caf061b3faafd0d2785cfd15/pykafka/protocol.py#L2434) should both be changed to the following:
```python
9: ApiVersionsSpec(9, 0, 1),
```
I've verified that this change causes all tests to pass except for [this one](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/814263f5cbf3d4a5caf061b3faafd0d2785cfd15/tests/pykafka/test_protocol.py#L902). To fix this test, you can print `str(msg)` [here](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/814263f5cbf3d4a5caf061b3faafd0d2785cfd15/tests/pykafka/test_protocol.py#L905) and copy the resulting bytestring into the `assertEqual` call. The string should be broken up like the existing one with linebreaks and indentation to make the different sections clearly visible. 

The protocol tests need a new class `TestOffsetCommitFetchAPIV2` with two functions `test_offset_fetch_request` and `test_offset_fetch_response` that validate the new v2 request/response. These new tests aren't required for this PR to be merged, but if they're not added here I'll add them after merge.

Again, if you'd like to add me as a contributor to this pull request, I'd be happy to make the tests pass, but please feel free to make the changes yourself.

Okay, interesting, and subtle.  This has to do with recent updates to pytest-cov that enable it, in a clever but hackish way, to collect coverage data on code run in subprocesses.  However, this hack breaks in a bad way if the path you give to the coveragerc file is a relative path, and your test changes directories before running the subprocess.

The breakage actually occurs very early on in the Python interpreter startup (for the subprocess) in site.py--Python 2.7 and up are able to ignore the exception and resume startup, but Python 2.6's site.py is less flexible and it will crash the whole interpeter startup if an unhandled exception occurs while processing a .pth file.

I'll report this to pytest-cov.  As a workaround, for now, I think I can pass a full path in for the config file.

FYI on the topic of `np.round`:

NumPy rounds correctly. Python's built-in round is incorrect. NumPy does NOT truncate, it follows the IEEE standard. All numbers exactly halfway between two integers are rounded to the nearest EVEN integer. This eliminates systematic positive error, which is present if you round up uniformly at x.5.

Use of np.round is more correct in almost every situation.

Adding a `move(parent_id, new_parent_id, collection_id, new_collection_id)` is probably the best option. And as you pointed out, it would be the only way to explicit run the sql command to explicitly lock the `records` and `deleted` tables. 

Regarding timestamps, unfortunately the current triggers are too vague, and would bump the timestamp even if we just update the `collection_id` column:
```
CREATE TRIGGER tgr_records_last_modified
BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE ON records
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE PROCEDURE bump_timestamp();

CREATE TRIGGER tgr_deleted_last_modified
BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE ON deleted
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE PROCEDURE bump_timestamp();
```

They should be replaced by this, which only targets the `data` column instead:

```
CREATE TRIGGER tgr_records_last_modified
BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE OF data ON records
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE PROCEDURE bump_timestamp();

CREATE TRIGGER tgr_deleted_last_modified
BEFORE INSERT ON deleted
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE PROCEDURE bump_timestamp();
```

Yeah, there are two separate issues here.  First is that numexpr does not understand unicode indeed:

```
In [25]: x = ra['f2'][:10]

In [26]: x
Out[26]: 
array([u'0', u'3', u'6', u'9', u'12', u'15', u'18', u'21', u'24', u'27'], 
      dtype='<U10')

In [27]: ne.evaluate("x == '9'")
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-27-282aa443feab> in <module>()
----> 1 ne.evaluate("x == '9'")

/home/faltet/software/numexpr/numexpr/necompiler.pyc in evaluate(ex, local_dict, global_dict, out, order, casting, **kwargs)
    787     # Create a signature
    788     signature = [(name, getType(arg)) for (name, arg) in
--> 789                  zip(names, arguments)]
    790 
    791     # Look up numexpr if possible.

/home/faltet/software/numexpr/numexpr/necompiler.pyc in getType(a)
    684     if kind == 'S':
    685         return bytes
--> 686     raise ValueError("unknown type %s" % a.dtype.name)
    687 
    688 

ValueError: unknown type unicode320
```

The second is that bcolz catches any possible exception throw by numexpr and tries to use the regular `eval()` in Python and this is why complains about ```NameError: name 'where' is not defined```.  This is why the next idiom works:

```
In [29]: ct.eval("f2 == '9'")[:10]
Out[29]: array([False, False, False,  True, False, False, False, False, False, False], dtype=bool)
```

which is equivalent to:

```
In [28]: ct.eval("f2 == '9'", vm="python")[:10]
Out[28]: array([False, False, False,  True, False, False, False, False, False, False], dtype=bool)
```

Note that you can use dask for performing operations too:

```
In [30]: ct.eval("f2 == '9'", vm="dask")[:10]
Out[30]: array([False, False, False,  True, False, False, False, False, False, False], dtype=bool)
```

Having said that, Unicode could be added to numexpr indeed, but that would be a long and painful process, so perhaps the above workaround works better for you.  If despite my warnings you are still interested in implementing unicode support, tell me again and I will try to come with more concrete suggestions.

In @joernhees branch, the simple test without a union (`simple_problematic_query`): 

```
select ?s ?o ?x where {
  ?x s:knows ?o .
  { BIND(?x as ?s) }
}
```

This is not meant to work. The group has it's own scope, and ?x is not visible inside. This also holds for UNION (which also induces it's own scope). 

This is exactly what bind07 tests: https://www.w3.org/2009/sparql/docs/tests/summary.html#bind-bind07

and also bind10: https://www.w3.org/2009/sparql/docs/tests/summary.html#bind-bind10

The tests don't have a simple BIND in a group example, but you can see here that groups and unions should be treated the same: https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#variableScope

`google-cloud-logging` cannot know the type of the payload message.  If you know the type, you can desrialize using `ProtobufEntry.parse_message`, e.g.:

```python
from google.appengine.logging.v1 import RequestLog

def unmarshall_request_log(entry):
    if entry.payload_pb.Is(RequestLog.DESCRIPTOR):
        message = RequestLog()
        protobuf_entry.parse_message(message)
        return message
```

To correctly specify a processor callback, it must be the third argument in the tuple, which means that you are compelled to provide a verbose name in the first position:

("Active", 'is_active', get_bool_check_mark)
```

It's one of the several reasons why the legacy syntax was a pain to work with :(

troposphere is a python implementation of the CloudFormation templating language.  If you see it in the docs in CloudFormation, you should be able to do the same in troposphere.  Notice how you use 'HostedZoneName'?  If you look at the CloudFormation documentation for RecordSet, you'll see that option is listed there, and they behave exactly the same:

http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-route53-recordset.html

You really shouldn't think of troposphere as a language of it's own.  It's just CloudFormation, but in python, rather than in JSON.  It has some extra helper features, but if you're looking for how to do something with a Resource Type or Property, your best bet is to consult the CloudFormation documentation for that ResourceType or Property.

I would vote against including a copy of `apply_bpe.py` into our repository and/or having multiple licenses in the repo. In the end, lexical constrained decoding is independent of the preprocessing required for a certain model. For the constrained decoding functionality I think its fine to assume that constraints have to be preprocessed in the same way as the input data. In my opinion, the kind kind of preprocessing used for seq2seq models is up to the user and is nothing we should enforce in Sockeye.
However, I do like having a tutorial on constrainted decoding that makes use of byte-pair encoded data. How about including a helper script that imports `apply_bpe` if on the PYTHONPATH into the tutorial directory for lexical constrained decoding? The tutorial then could also include the instructions how to clone/obtain the original `apply_bpe.py` script and how to add it to the PYTHONPATH?

It is different from the standard CloudFormation syntax. In SAM, "MyLambdaFunction" is the logical id of an AWS::Serverless::Function, and this corresponds to *multiple* standard CloudFormation resources: an AWS::Lambda::Function, an AWS::Lambda::Version, and an AWS::Lambda::Alias (see the in-progress new documentation [here](https://awslabs.github.io/serverless-application-model/internals/generated_resources.html#with-autopublishalias-property). But you only have one logical id. So to give access to these "hidden" resources, SAM is using this special "subresource" syntax of .Version and .Alias.

Presets introduced here: https://projects.invisionapp.com/share/84BSA4H23#/screens/247458260

The presets are a (new) way of providing a short hand for facility configuration options.

The aim of storing them is if we add a new facility configuration option, we have a default that is consistent with previously expressed user preference.

Previously, there were no presets, and users would just select facility configurations after onboarding (or just be left with the defaults). As desirable defaults may differ depending on the context (as implemented by the preset) we store the preset to set the defaults right in the case we add new options.

Here’s what I "sort of know", or at least my understanding of how design spaces differ in MutatorMath and Glyphs: 

Both **Superpolator** / **MutatorMath** and **OpenType Variations** iterate through each axis and determine the interpolated coordinates per each axis individually, step by step. This allows an arbitrary number of axes, but at a cost — predictable results are given if the masters are at "corresponding" locations, like you can see on the below image. In other words, in MutatorMath, the design space always needs to be divided into rectangular areas: 

![](http://new.superpolator.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/10/sp3_mainWindow.gif)

**Glyphs** is different: it throws all the masters from all the axes into the interpolation mix at the same time, so with each additional axis, the complexity of the equation grows by a degree. This allows greater flexibility in the arrangement of masters, but makes it much more difficult to add more axes. This is why Glyphs has the limit of 3 axes. In Glyphs, different equations are used depending on whether you have 1 axis, or 2 axes or 3. 

My understanding is that in MutatorMath, and I think also in OT Variations, your designspace always needs to be divided into rectangular areas. In other words, in Glyphs, you can have a setup like this: 

![](https://glyphsapp.com/content/2-tutorials/52-multiple-masters-part-1-setting-up-masters/interpolation-4.png)

but in MutatorMath or OT Variations, you cannot.

So, with all the changes we made to py-evm/py-rlp/py-trie things got significantly better, but `JournalDB` is still the biggest offender and makes block importing just plain unfeasible (because it does an extra `LevelDB.get()` every time we update/delete a key) on a fully-synced ropsten DB. Here's a 
[new stats file](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/files/1908308/block-import-stats.txt) that aggregates the profiling stats of only `Chain.import_block()` for 8 consecutive blocks (unlike the previous one, which was profiling the networking code as well).

Given the above, I've been thinking about ways to fix that and I believe we might be able to use [LevelDB snapshots](http://plyvel.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#Snapshot) in our `JournalDB` to avoid the extra `LevelDB.get()` on every key update/delete. We'd change `JournalDB` to add only the keys to the journal, and then on `.revert()` we read their values from the snapshot and set those values to the actual DB. That way there's no overhead on DB operations unless we have to revert to a snapshot.

Can you guys see foresee any issues with that approach? Do you think it's worth pursuing?

had similar issue here.  Now I'm using a workaround.  Find the repo of .tar.gz of the package you are trying to install. You can go to the channel URLs and search. Download the package directly to the .pkgs directory of conda in your home directory. The .pkgs could be located at ~/.conda/.pkgs or ~/anaconda_env/.pkgs. Also add the package download URL to the urls file inside the .pkgs folder. Then run conda install again, it will be installed successfully.

Thanks for the response @kalefranz. I am getting the same error even after specifying the command which you mentioned. Please help me in solving this issue

The way that `conda list` works is the following: It looks at all conda packages, and then (if pip is even installed) it calls out to `pip` and adds the "pip" packages, for which there is no conda package name.  It is true that this is Python specific behavior.  Removing `pip` will make `conda list` only list the conda packages.
xref: https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6147

The whole point of this discussion is to figure out how to get virtualenv working with the python interpreter that conda installed.  By using `virtualenv -p`, you are forcing it to use a different python interpreter.

Is there any benefit to installing `virtualenv` with conda and then using the system python interpreter?  (Personally, I don't have much experience with `virtualenv`, so I could be confused.  But it seems wrong to do that.

> The user workflow in Anaconda Platform as it's shaping up currently doesn't really have direct use of conda or conda env or environment.yml in it - nothing stops you from using them, but the beginning user docs would not need to go into them to get people started.

> So we have the two commands that are in the docs - at least the command line docs - anaconda and anaconda-project. The distinction between anaconda and anaconda-project is that anaconda affects the Anaconda platform server and anaconda-project affects your local project directory.
> 
> It's also possible to work on a notebook with only the GUI and no command line tools. In Jupyter there will be a "Packages" list and you can add packages to the list ... there's no manual environment manipulation. > 
> The way we're doing this the workflow can be something like 1) click "new notebook", 2) see notebook now open, 3) click "add package" to add packages you need, 4) work on notebook, 5) click the save or share button to share with colleagues. Here you are supposing that compression/decompression is a very expensive operation, and it is not so, at least on modern CPUs with several cores.  Also, bcolz is highly optimized for performing operations in a block-wise way for better cache usage.  For example, with an CPU with 4 physical cores I am getting this:

```
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: import bcolz

In [3]: N = int(1e7); ct = bcolz.fromiter(((i, i * 3, i * 2.) for i in xrange(N)), dtype='i4,f4,f8', count=N)

In [4]: ct
Out[4]: 
ctable((10000000,), [('f0', '<i4'), ('f1', '<f4'), ('f2', '<f8')])
  nbytes: 152.59 MB; cbytes: 6.33 MB; ratio: 24.11
  cparams := cparams(clevel=5, shuffle=1, cname='lz4', quantize=0)
[(0, 0.0, 0.0) (1, 3.0, 2.0) (2, 6.0, 4.0) ...,
 (9999997, 29999992.0, 19999994.0) (9999998, 29999994.0, 19999996.0)
 (9999999, 29999996.0, 19999998.0)]

In [5]: %timeit ct.eval('f0 + f1 + f2')
10 loops, best of 3: 55.5 ms per loop

# Using pure numpy here:
In [6]: ra = ct[:]

In [7]: ra
Out[7]: 
array([(0, 0.0, 0.0), (1, 3.0, 2.0), (2, 6.0, 4.0), ...,
       (9999997, 29999992.0, 19999994.0),
       (9999998, 29999994.0, 19999996.0), (9999999, 29999996.0, 19999998.0)], 
      dtype=[('f0', '<i4'), ('f1', '<f4'), ('f2', '<f8')])

In [8]: %timeit ra['f0'] + ra['f1'] + ra['f2']
10 loops, best of 3: 72.1 ms per loop
```

Doing the same operation  without compression is actually slower:

```
In [10]: bcolz.defaults.cparams['clevel'] = 0

In [11]: N = int(1e7); ct = bcolz.fromiter(((i, i * 3, i * 2.) for i in xrange(N)), dtype='i4,f4,f8', count=N)

In [12]: ct
Out[12]: 
ctable((10000000,), [('f0', '<i4'), ('f1', '<f4'), ('f2', '<f8')])
  nbytes: 152.59 MB; cbytes: 153.50 MB; ratio: 0.99
  cparams := cparams(clevel=0, shuffle=1, cname='lz4', quantize=0)
[(0, 0.0, 0.0) (1, 3.0, 2.0) (2, 6.0, 4.0) ...,
 (9999997, 29999992.0, 19999994.0) (9999998, 29999994.0, 19999996.0)
 (9999999, 29999996.0, 19999998.0)]

In [13]: %timeit ct.eval('f0 + f1 + f2')
10 loops, best of 3: 65.2 ms per loop

```

Regarding lazy operations, yes, depending on your needs that could be a win. Dask does this already, so not sure we should replicate it in bcolz.  bcolz is more meant to let the user specify the complete amount of operations in a single expression and then execute in one go (which is usually very efficient).

I think I figured out what is going on. The server at `ws-feed-public.sandbox.gdax.com` implements [SNI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Server_Name_Indication) which requires a hostname to be sent at the start of the handshaking process. For example, we can connect like this:

```bash
openssl -connect ws-feed-public.sandbox.gdax.com -servername ws-feed-public.sandbox.gdax.com`
```
[ssl.wrap_socket](https://docs.python.org/3/library/ssl.html#ssl.wrap_socket) does not accept a `server_hostname` argument, but [ssl.SSLContext.wrap_socket](https://docs.python.org/3/library/ssl.html#ssl.SSLContext.wrap_socket) does. I can successfully connect by changing [the code here](https://github.com/wildfoundry/dataplicity-lomond/blob/master/lomond/session.py#L109) to:

```python
sock = ssl.SSLContext().wrap_socket(sock, server_hostname=self._address[0])
```

But this seems pretty hacky.

> I'm not sure I've correctly understood your solution. You want to split the multinomial feature into N separate 0/1 features and model them with a Gaussian? This might work, because in the limit everything tends to look like a Gaussian, but I'm not completely sure.

I apologize for deleting my solution in my previous comment because I don't think I've already completely thought over it. Now that you responded to that I'd like to clarify more.

Yes, you're most right but I think it's supposed to be N*2 Gaussian nodes. For example, supposing we have 3 multinomial states, I would use 6 continuous nodes to model them, which represents ```[state_1_false, state_1 true, state_2_false, state_2 true, state_3_false, state_3 true]```

For example, if the observed state is 2, then the feature vector is [0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0]. This can ensure the learned mean is exactly either 0 or 1, instead of a continuous value in between.

> So if it works badly, you might want to try an HMM which uses Gaussians for some of the features and Multinomials for the remaining features.

I see. Thank you for the hint.

Evaluating unsupervised models is tricky and, unfortunately, I'm not aware of an efficiency criteria for Viterbi predictions. 

I mostly work with posterior state probabilities, because they can be used to estimate and control FDR for model predictions (see reference in #25). The method is only applicable if you have a null (binary) hypothesis. E.g. if the states are `{SIGNAL, NOISE}` then the reasonable null hypothesis might be "the true state is NOISE".

Today I thought about why ETag is better than date check again.
I searched on the web and I found out people mentioned that files extracted from compressed files would have a Last-Modification-Date in the past (before they are packed). If I extract files into the /static/ folder and overwrite something with the same name, it would cause a problem. 
Besides, some people said sometimes a file of the same address could be different files on the server (depends on user settings)
For instance:
If user setting of color theme on a website changed from ‘red’ to ‘green’, then ‘/pics/background.jpg’ may changed from actually returning ‘/pics/red/backgroud.jpg’ to ‘/pics/green/backgroud.jpg’. If-Modified-Since would cause problem in this case, and ETag will work as expected
Maybe ETag support would be a necessary one?
But I don’t know how to implement a ETag support. (If someone share me the ETag algorithm I’m happy to help)
Fortunately we have @hubo1016 here who knows the implementation. We could expect a patch from him. He said that ETag would not impact performance, and does not involve hashing. I believe it would be a good choice to have it.

OK, here is a summary of the past and current state:

In 4.0:

* `NotebookApp.config_manager_class` is a configurable that the notebook app uses to instantiate `self.config_manager`.
* This instances is used in the config handler (it gets passed to the tornado settings and ends up as an attribute on the handler) to set/get config from the frontend. But the `config_dir` attribute is not used in that API.
* In 4.0, we hard-code the `config_dir` attribute of the config manager when the `NotebookApp` instantiates it. Because of this, @damianavila can swap out the configurable `config_manager_class`, but can't set which config dir is used.

In 4.1 master:

* `NotebookApp.config_manager_class` is still used to instantiate the class that is used in the config handler.
* But we no longer hard-code the `config_dir` attribute at that location in the `NotebookApp`.
* Now the default comes from the `ConfigManager` class itself, which has a `_config_dir_default` method that has been there all along, but wasn't being used because we were passing it in.

My proposal for 4.2:

* I pull a sleight of hand to load config from multiple directories in a backwards compatible manner.
* My `ConfigManager` class has the exactly same API that is used by the handlers.
* **But**, it doesn't have a `config_dir` attribute at all because it ends up loading from multiple directories.

My conclusion:

* If we consider `config_dir` to be part of the public API in 4.0, then my entire approach falls apart (it is backwards incompatible).
* But because of the bug that was in 4.0, it was mostly impossible to pass in a custom config manager that uses a different `config_dir`.
* Because of that bug, I am not sure we should consider any of this as being public API in 4.0.
* **But** the bug fix of @damianavila does make it all public (in current master).
* If someone uses a custom `ConfigManager` that subclasses the one from 4.1, my 4.2 approach will likely break their code.

Summary: a bug in 4.0 essentially hides an API from users/devs in a way that allows me to change it without breaking backwards compatibility. The bug fix in 4.1 will make my 4.2 proposal backwards incompatible.

But, I don't think anyone is using any of this stuff other than @damianavila et al. So if he is fine moving forward with the current state of 4.1 *and* is fine with my proposal for 4.2 that will break his 4.1 stuff, then I am OK.

My main concern here is that I don't want us to be prevented from improving the nbextension situation for users in 4.2, just because of an obscure, unused API.

Actually I need to perform some migrations that fall outside the scope of the OpenUpgrade project, so the idea behind this is to be able to make this library more reusable for those scenarios.

When writing a pre script, I found that if the whole script is not bundled inside a transaction, you would end up having to restore the database to test the script again, which makes the developer lose a lot of time.

Please don't confuse the `with` with a `try`. If an exception happens, it will be effectively raised. The only difference is that all sentences inside the block will be discarded.

According to my tests, when that exception is raised, it makes Odoo retry the migration in an endless loop. Without this fix, each try will yield a different failure because the transaction is not rolled back; with it, all tries will yield the same error. This allows you to just fix the script and reboot Odoo to make it continue.

Besides, AFAIK savepoints can be found inside other savepoints. If you say OpenUpgrade uses one transaction for the whole pre+post process in OpenUpgrade, then in the worst case this patch should not hurt, don't you think?

I like this idea a lot. My proposed implementation would be more than just documentation however given the complexity of repositories such as ``tools-devteam`` and ``tools-iuc``.

Implementation Suggestion:

Implement a ``planemo hook_init`` that will add the hook script to a repository and create a ``.planemo_hook.yml`` file if not present. The hook script will simply run a ``hook_check`` command that looks at the optional ``.planemo_hook.yml`` file and does the linting described within.

An absent ``.planemo_hook.yml`` file corresponds to just effectively running ``planemo shed_lint --tools --recursive`` over the whole repository - which will lints all repositories and all tools it finds within them. This will fail for tools-devteam and tools-iuc still - so ``.planemo_hook.yml`` can be used to do more targeted testing.

```
# Look at all shed artifacts but skip digging into tools.
shed:
 - paths: .
   recursive: true
   tools: false
tool:
 # Do full linting on select repositories that are up to date.
 - paths: tools/bwa,tool_collections/vcflib/vcf2tsv
 # Do less strict linting on other repositories
 - paths: tools/bowtie2 
   skip_formatting: false
 # Simply look for serious errors on older repositories
 - paths: tools/emboss_5/emboss_5
   fail_level: error
```

In addition to making things more lax ``.planemo.yml`` could be used to make things more stringent as well - specifically I am imagining a ``xsd: true`` field that would enable the new tool XSD checks on discovered tools.

This is still an issue, especially with more complex code.  You may not wait on an async task until test teardown.  I ran into an issue where a background task was silently throwing an exception causing the test to hang.  I was able to expose the exception by setting the exception handler:

```python
@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_foo(event_loop):
    def handle_async_exception(loop, ctx):
        pytest.fail("Exception in async task: {0}".format(ctx['exception']))
    event_loop.set_exception_handler(handle_async_exception)
```

It seems like this should be built into pytest-asyncio, however.

Seems like the problem here is in `mock.patch` itself, not `pytest-asyncio`. 
See Martiusweb/asynctest#5 for details. The package `asynctest` provides a fixed implementation of `mock.patch` which works just fine and above tests are passing with it.

I am closing this issue since it is not `pytest-asyncio` issue.

@joohoi is actually working on this, in part to make support for DNS challenges practical.  I don't think he's decided on a a design yet, though some of the options under discussion include:

* Run a "Step 1" command that gives you a challenge, and then a "Step 2" command when you've deployed it.  Challenge: how to serialise and save state so that the client knows where to pick up from.
* Call the client with two hook functions, one of which is to be run when a challenge is ready, the other when it has succeeded.  Challenge: does this mean that the user has to write three separate scripts, one for each hook and one to call the client with the hooks?

Another option that @bmw and I talked about recently is:

* Implement hooks, but encourage the user write a fancy script that the client can itself invoke, something like this:
```
DeployChallenge() {
   CHALLENGE_URL=$1
   CHALLENGE_CONTENT=$2
   # do stuff
}

CleanupChallenge() {
   CHALLENGE_URL=$1
   CHALLENGE_CONTENT=$2
   # undo stuff
}

if [ -z "$WITHIN_LE_CLIENT" ] ; then
   letsencrypt certonly --script --script-challenge HTTP01 --challenge-hook ". $0 ; DeployChallenge"  --cleanup-hook ". $0 ; CleanupChallenge"
fi
```
That's not terrible, but it has the problems that the client invocation is a bit long and weird.  And would even need extra stuff (`$0` -> `\"$0\") to work if the script had a space in its path. Which leads to:

* Do the same thing as above, but bake in the bash function names and add some syntactic sugar.  So the script would look like:

```
DeployHTTP01Challenge() {
   CHALLENGE_URL=$1
   CHALLENGE_CONTENT=$2
   # do stuff
}

CleanupHTTP01Challenge() {
   CHALLENGE_URL=$1
   CHALLENGE_CONTENT=$2
   # undo stuff
}

if [ -z "$WITHIN_LE_CLIENT" ] ; then
   letsencrypt certonly --script --script-challenge HTTP01 --script-hooks "$0"
fi

The 3-letter version works only because your local time zone happens to be EST and we're able to pick up the names `EST` and `EDT` from `time.tzname`. Otherwise we have no way of mapping `Eastern Daylight Time` to a specific time zone.

Normally, I'd say just add whatever zone name -> zone mappings you want in `tzinfos` and it will pick that up, but unfortunately the way we're identifying "possible zone names" precludes anything longer than ~4 characters, and expanding that definition has some knock-on effects that create other unfortunate or ambiguous parses. At some point we will rework how datetimes are parsed and this will be fixed.

It looks like the problem is that Python thinks the file system encoding is 'US-ASCII', when it fact it's UTF-8.

Digging in a bit, it looks like Mac and Windows have a standard encoding that Python knows about.  I test mostly on Mac, so it always works for me:

```
/* The default encoding used by the platform file system APIs
   Can remain NULL for all platforms that don't have such a concept
*/
#if defined(MS_WINDOWS) && defined(HAVE_USABLE_WCHAR_T)
const char *Py_FileSystemDefaultEncoding = "mbcs";
#elif defined(__APPLE__)
const char *Py_FileSystemDefaultEncoding = "utf-8";
#else
const char *Py_FileSystemDefaultEncoding = NULL; /* use default */
#endif
```

Testing on Linux, I find that setting the LANG environment variable will tell Python what encoding to use by default.  Try setting LANG to `en_US.UTF-8`, and see if that fixes the problem.  This is what happens on Debian:

```
$ unset LANG
$ python
Python 2.7.3 (default, Jun 21 2016, 18:38:19) 
[GCC 4.7.2] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import sys; sys.getfilesystemencoding()
'ANSI_X3.4-1968'
>>> 
$ export LANG=en_US.UTF-8
$ python
Python 2.7.3 (default, Jun 21 2016, 18:38:19) 
[GCC 4.7.2] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import sys; sys.getfilesystemencoding()
'UTF-8'
>>> 
```

1. In laser, execute and save the states of 1 basic block down the "always false" path
2. If that block ends in a revert or invalid instruction it's assumed to be an assert or require failure

I just added the capability to include two options: global and lang (in [Highchart.setOptions](http://api.highcharts.com/highcharts#lang.thousandsSep)). However, it seems like there are something set only by these two.

You can use these two as other options by set_option/set_dict_options method. To add commas, you need to set thousandsSep in lang. You can check this [discussion](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/29209491/highcharts-format-all-numbers-with-comma) for how to construct the option.

The other issue though at the right-hand end of my graph with negative transit elevations. I added some code to brute-force the transit. The script below shows the brute-forced values on the second line of output and the get_next_passes() value on the third line. Looks like the transit calculation in get_next_passes() occasionally gets lost and returns the wrong time. The script is setup to show an example of this. Hope that helps.

````
from pyorbital import tlefile
from pyorbital.orbital import Orbital
from datetime import datetime
from datetime import timedelta
import re

TLE_FILE_NAME = 'iridium-tle.txt'

HORIZON = 40

# altitude (km) above sea-level and lat lon of the observer
ALTITUDE_ASL = 0.5
LON = 170.556
LAT = -43.368

SATELLITE_LIST = []

tlefile.TLE_URLS = ('https://celestrak.com/NORAD/elements/iridium.txt', )
tlefile.fetch(TLE_FILE_NAME)


def get_satellite_names():
    # read the element file to get a list of names for the satellites
    with open(TLE_FILE_NAME, 'rt') as f:
        for l in f:
            if not re.match('^[12] ', l) and not l.startswith('DUMMY MASS'):
                name = l.strip()
                SATELLITE_LIST.append(name)


def timerange(start_date, end_date):
    for n in range(int ((end_date - start_date).seconds)):
        yield start_date + timedelta(0, n)


def find_transit(orb, t0, t1):

    max_el = -999
    max_az = -999
    t_max  = -999

    # hack to handle case where SV just touches the horizon
    if t0 + timedelta(0, 2) < t1:

        for t in timerange(t0, t1):
            az, el = orb.get_observer_look(t, LON, LAT, ALTITUDE_ASL)
            if el > max_el:
                max_el = el
                max_az = az
                t_max = t

        return t_max, max_az, max_el

    else:
        return t0, orb.get_observer_look(t0, LON, LAT, ALTITUDE_ASL)
  

def do_calc(d, sat_name, duration):
    orb = Orbital(sat_name, TLE_FILE_NAME)
    passes = orb.get_next_passes(d, duration, LON, LAT, ALTITUDE_ASL, horizon=HORIZON)

    for p in passes:
        rise_az, rise_el = orb.get_observer_look(p[0], LON, LAT, ALTITUDE_ASL)
        transit_time, transit_az, transit_el = find_transit(orb, p[0], p[1])
        set_az, set_el = orb.get_observer_look(p[1], LON, LAT, ALTITUDE_ASL)

        tr_az, tr_el = orb.get_observer_look(p[2], LON, LAT, ALTITUDE_ASL)

        alert = ' <<<<<<<<<<<<<' if tr_el < 0.01 or not (p[2] > p[0] < p[1]) else ''

        print(sat_name)
        print(p[0], rise_az, rise_el)
        print(transit_time, transit_az, transit_el)
        print(p[2], tr_az, tr_el, alert)
        print(p[1], set_az, set_el)
        print()

do_calc(datetime(2018, 3, 12, 14, 30, 0), 'IRIDIUM 7 [+]',  2)

#     except:
#         pass

````

> Actually tombstones should works as non existing record, the record has been deleted so it doesn't exists anymore. The tombstones is just an indication that the record once existed but if you create it again it is a totally brand new record.

Actually thinking through it with the changes made on #1102 I agree with that. We can follow this approach and simplify a bit some parts of the code.

 My main concern for this was what happens if:
1. At timestamp 1, user A creates a record R.
2. At timestamp 2, user B fetches and deletes the record R.
3. At timestamp 3, user A uses put again over the record record R `If-Match: "1"`.

From my understanding the expected messages should be.
1. Create
2. Delete
3. Precondition failed 

Both before and after #1102 we would get the same messages, but from different sources. Before we would raise because we checked tombstones for `If-Match`, now it will raise because `If-Match` will always fail on non-existing records.

Currently at every batch we reduce the number of models by a fixed fraction, regardless of the size of the batch. `certbot-auto` runs `apt-get` without the `-y` flag intentionally, so that users are not surprised by changes to their system.  `certbot-auto` looks for the `-n` or `--non-interactive` flags and adds `-y` to the apt call in those cases.  However it doesn't currently look for the `renew` subcommand which also implies `-n`.  It should do that.

Also, we should possibly avoid re-running `apt-get` if we've already done that and the packages are present (which would have mitigated the problem that motivated #3955 too).

@yuvipanda we ended up copying ldapauthenticator and making our own modifications. It seemed to me that the way we need to authenticate and dig information out from LDAP wouldn't be easily supported and made configurable.

Here are the essential parts of our authentication mechanism:

```python
        # Try to authenticate the user
        user_conn = ldap3.Connection(
            server,
            user=self.bind_dn_template.format(username=username),
            password=password)
        if not user_conn.bind():
            return None  # invalid password

        # User authentication succeeded, get the Unix UID and optionally
        # check group authorization
        conn = ldap3.Connection(server,
                                user=self.service_account_dn,
                                password=self.service_account_password)

        if conn.bind():
            # In some cases, like AD, we don't bind with the DN, and need to
            # discover it.
            conn.search(search_base=self.user_search_base,
                        search_scope=ldap3.SUBTREE,
                        search_filter='({userattr}={username})'.format(
                            userattr=self.user_attribute,
                            username=username),
                        attributes=[self.user_attribute, 'uidNumber'])

            if len(conn.response) == 0:
                self.log.warn('User with %s=%s not found in directory',
                              self.user_attribute, username)
                return None
            userdn = conn.response[0]['dn']
            entry = conn.entries[0]

            # The user attributes will be stored in users.state in the
            # database by our customized Docker spawner
            self.user_attributes = entry.entry_attributes_as_dict
            return username
        else:
            self.log.warn('Cannot bind to LDAP server using service account %s',
                          self.service_account_dn)
            return None
```

The tricky part is that we will only use the `_index` piece for `_output` and ignore all others.

@JainVikas What is the exported pipeline? I made up a exported pipeline example based on your question as below. The scikit-learn Pipeline object has the API to access `feature_importances_` in the intermediate step of a Pipeline. But it is noted that the `feature_importances_` in step N would be for features from step N-1 instead of input features for the Pipeline. So if there are feature selection/stacking estimators to exclude/add feature before step N then the `feature_importances_` should have different feature number with input features (X).

```python
from sklearn.datasets import load_digits
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler
from sklearn.tree import ExtraTreeClassifier

digits = load_digits()
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(digits.data, digits.target,
                                                    train_size=0.75, test_size=0.25)


exported_pipeline = make_pipeline(
    MinMaxScaler(),
    ExtraTreeClassifier()
)

exported_pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
# print steps
print(exported_pipeline.steps)
# getting the  feature importances from step 2 extratreeclassifier
exported_pipeline.named_steps['extratreeclassifier'].feature_importances_```

@lukaszlacinski, When the DFK creates a DataFuture, the DataFutures's parent is an AppFuture, and it's parent is a Future that tracks the task sent to the executor. In this case, we are creating a DataFutures which does not have an AppFuture intermediary. 

To fix this we just need to check if the parent is an AppFuture or Future and set parent appropriately.

There are 2 ways I see we can change this:
1. Define `word_tokenize` as a callable object. Pros : nice and clean. Cons : you have to change the API for the end user.
2. Define a constant global variable `THAI_WORDS = Trie(get_data())` in `tokenize.newmm`. Pros : very easy, nearly no changes. Cons : not extremely pythonic + people who give their own data would still have to load the default one once (but I think it's neglectable)

I went with the second point as changing the API is probably not a good idea for other users.

 ```python
txt = """...paste wiki..."""

print(len(txt.splitlines()))
# 539

### Global Variable - NEWMM ###
%%timeit
out1 = [pythainlp.tokenize.word_tokenize(line,engine="newmm") for line in txt.splitlines()]
# 68 ms ± 374 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10 loops each)
```

So it's now the same speed as ICU when tokenizing lists (with only one line added) 👍

    Out[2]: True

If this doesn't make sense to you, I suggest reading up on how floating point works (IEEE 754).
